pages.full_text:
John's birthday August 19th
We drove to the Tapestry. It was marvellous.
The headphones guide at the end took us through
at just the right pace. We saw the
cathedral.
     We stopped at Port en Bessin to buy

[photo of the Bayeux Tapestry — caption text along right margin, rotated:]
William the Conqueror & his half-brother
Bishop Odo, after they arrived at Pevensey
[?March] The bishop had fish because it's a Friday
photo_captions: William the Conqueror & his half-brother Bishop Odo, after they arrived at Pevensey [?March] The bishop had fish because it's a Friday
pages.notes: Page features a glued-in photograph/postcard of the Bayeux Tapestry (feast scene). Caption is written vertically along the right margin of the photo. 'John's birthday August 19th' is the heading — no year visible on this page; volume B covers 2003–2019 so year cannot be determined from this spread alone. The word before 'The bishop had fish' could read 'March' but context suggests it may be a different word — possibly 'Marvellous' or another adjective.

pages.full_text:
John's birthday cake. Unlike the ribbon
development further East, it's a nice small
town.
     On to __Longues__. We had lunch at
the carpark & looked at the German gun
emplacements & a look-out post. It
could see all the way from Cherbourg
almost to __Pegasus Bridge__, but no
radar. & telephone wires cut by
bombing.
     __Pointe du Hoc__ was the last war
place we visited. The Rangers took it with
tremendous losses. There are craters all over
the ground — the land around is scrubby &
barren, so farmers haven't put the soil
together to farm again.
     We passed a German war cemetery.
There are 5 stone crosses to each plot &
stone squares in the grass. Charles said that
unlike the Allies, they share stones. 40,000
German soldiers died in this area.
     We went to [he] Clerk supermarket.
Simon bought lots of wine. We got gas
[h.p.] St. Low
pages.notes: Continuation of the August 19th entry (John's birthday). The trip is clearly to Normandy D-Day sites. 'he Clerk supermarket' likely reads 'le Clerc supermarket' (Leclerc, a French supermarket chain) — the diarist may have written 'he' for 'le' or it could be a ligature. The final line appears to read 'h.p. St. Low' or 'n.p. St. Lo' — 'St. Lo' (Saint-Lô) is a town in Normandy and is the more likely reading; 'h.p.' may stand for 'heading to' or be an abbreviation. No year is stated on this page; year cannot be determined from content alone.
